sentences.text: Yes.
I'm-- I born 10th of June, 1922 in Vinnitsa.
I am now 75 years old.
I mean 74.
My maiden name is [INAUDIBLE] Beri.
B-E-I-R-E.
What city?
In Vinnitsa.
Vinnitsa is a large city.
I grow up in Vinnitsa till I was about nine years of age, and then my father was an army officer.
So And they sent him north to Leningrad, where we lived up to 1938-- '39.
And-- yes?
Yes.
My mother is maiden name was Gefter.
She married my father, Beri David-- David.
My mother was born and Vinnitsa too.
My father was born in a little town, Mariupol.
It's all in Ukraine.
I don't remember the date of death--
--of birth of them, but do-- I do remember when they died.
So--
I have two sisters.
The middle's-- I'm the eldest.
My middle sister is [?
Raissa-- ?] like Rachel.
And my youngest sister is Alexsandra, [?
Shura ?].
The-- my middle sister born in 1920-- '24 in December.
And my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] youngest sister born in 1928 in September.
My last address before the war was [NON-ENGLISH].
We lived in [NON-ENGLISH].
We lived in blocks of flats for Army officers, which we called [RUSSIAN].
It's in Russian.
I wouldn't know how to translate it in-- in German.
It's 100 flats, approximately, and they was occupied only by Army officers, Red Army officers.
That was my last place where we lived.
My father was an army officer.
His rank was captain. it was just before the second war break.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we lived in Leningrad.
Because of his sickness, we were sent from Leningrad south in [NON-ENGLISH], which is Krasnodar.
And--
The real position-- I just say army officer because I don't know what his position was in Army, OK?
I know his rank, and I know where he worked, and that's about all.
Yes.
He was in administration-- [NON-ENGLISH] administration.
Yes.
So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do you want me right before the war or just from the beginning?
What home?
My childhood was very good because of my father having position in army.
And I don't know whether you know.
In those days, army officers and army families was in the first league-- least in first place.
Everything was done for the army officers, army people, and of course, their families.
So my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] childhood, particularly, was good because we had the opportunity to not only to go to school and to educate ourselves.
But because we belong to sort of house of Red Army, there was a lot of sport activities, dances, singing, and all sort of things, which is for nothing.
And of course, my parents gave us all what we can do to make us enjoy our childhood.
It's not only me, but just as much my sisters.
They enjoy all these things.
Oh, we were in orchestra.
We were in dancing.
We were in all sort of sports.
We have a basketball team.
We have a family basketball team that was three-- two families-- three of us and a family Kowalski.
We had first place in Krasnodar.
And then in all district Krasnodar.
We have not only basketball-- volleyball-- the same thing, so--
So to tell you the truth, in my childhood my parents gave us some much that all would come later on helped me to get through, because later on I have very bad memories.
And I thank my parents for all the things they they-- well, they were able, and they give us.
Not everybody had this in Russia.
[SOBBING] There was many.
In Army, I wouldn't say there was many.
But my father was very likable.
They love him.
And we didn't feel much as a Jewish.
I have to tell you, we knew we're Jewish.
We were in passport written Jewish.
And my-- except that we couldn't have old traditions, Jewish tradition, in our family.
But we were lucky, because we have grandparents, which were traditional.
And they give us a lot of knowledge about Jewish tradition.
We knew Pesach.
We knew Hanukkah.
We knew Purim.
We knew [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Rosh Hashanah.
We knew all about these because we lived with my mother's mother, who had-- who had seven children.
And she-- by herself, because her husband died very early.
[INAUDIBLE] and she lived opposite where we lived.
It was in Vinnitsa.
And Hanukkah gelt-- I remember like today.
When Hanukkah come, she was running around to give all her grandchildren and grand-grandchildren money, because that was tradition.
When Purim come, she was backing baking hamentashen, and she brought home.
I remember for Pesach, we had to go to my father's family, because his father was very traditional.
And they used to bake matzos.
So I remember like today from matzos, they have to do the matzo meal.
So he was standing and crushing this matzo to give us matzo meal, so that the way have [NON-ENGLISH] and all those things.
So that's about all what we could have in Jewish tradition.
Not in Russia.
We didn't have any-- like, Jewish organization, you mean?
Oh yes, my-- my father was in party-- communist party.
We youngsters-- there was a youth-- a youth organization [INAUDIBLE].
We belonged there too.
We were very patriotic.
We didn't have much to complain about, because when it was very bad in '33-- I remember like today-- we had everything because my father was in army.
Pardon?
I-- I-- I attend primary school.
I attend high school.
And I had two years university.
[SIGHS] You have to know Marxism.
You have to know the line of the party-- Communist party.
That was in all schools and universities.
You have to learn, like, history.
I was a history teacher.
I have to learn history.
But I have to have history of Russia, history of communism, history of this.
That's how it was.
And-- and then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it didn't bother us, because we born to think about a day, to live about-- like this, and it was OK with us.
For two years.
And when-- I was-- I was doing-- [SIGHS] that's a story.
You see, I finished my high school in 1940.
To get into university in 1940-- yes, 1940-- to get into university then, they didn't have exams.
The best sort of-- how you call it-- that is-- when you get from high school?
Graduation-- the best graduate people were taking university.
With me was this-- I wasn't a bad student.
I rather was a good student.
But when my exams finished, I had one three, which is no good.
Four or five was good.
But three to have-- that's bad.
So I couldn't get to university.
So what I did in the first year when-- '41-- I have to go to university, I decided if they don't take me, so what I'd do-- I do [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] assistant-- medical assistant course.
And I put my name.
And I was-- but in the 1st September-- no 5th of September-- the 1st September start the university.
On 5th of September, I come home.
Mom said, you got a letter from university.
I said, how come?
And I took university.
And it was for physical education department I had.
Because of my sport and being known in Krasnodar all over, they said that we will take you at university in the course you wanted.
But you have to come from this sport club-- sport organization, which was [NON-ENGLISH].
That's House of Red Army-- from that to teacher, because that was a teacher in university.
And I, of course, said OK.
And that's how I from the first-- from about 10 days of university, I went to university.
For two years.
Now, '41 and '42.
At home?
You see, I never-- we could understand a bit of Yiddish, when Mom and Dad would speak.
They didn't speak much, but if they wanted so that we don't know, the children, so they spoke between them Yiddish.
We, of course, never learned and never knew.
So that was only Russian and Ukrainian.
[SIGHS] Oh, you see, we didn't know much, because in 1939, Hitler signed the contract that he will never invade Russian.
And all of a sudden, in June 1941, he invade Ukraine, which is a part of Russia, and start to bomb and all sort of thing.
Of course, the Russian people didn't like that.
And me, of course not.
We didn't know what's going on till later on what Hitler does to people, what he do with people-- doing.
So we learn only later.
And that help us to survive-- me, my mother.
My father was there the first day of the war.
He was in the army, and was in the front.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
We--
Before the war, as I said, anti-Semitism always exists, more in Ukraine than in center Russia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I can't say that we, as a family, did sort of see it every time we would all go wherever we are.
Because?
We were mostly with army people, army families.
And at school, we didn't feel like we are Jews.
We didn't hear it.
In Leningrad-- nothing.
So it wasn't as bad.
We learn about more later on.
Maybe because we were young, we didn't play attention to the sort of things.
We-- maybe that's why we don't remember this all.
But to tell you the truth, that's the only thing that I wouldn't say was so bad.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
[DRINKS] Not much.
I now that my-- my father was in the army when they went to [NON-ENGLISH]. and this was taking.
So he was at this campaign.
Then some part of Poland went to Russia.
That much we knew.
But of course, what the German-- the Hitler is no good.
We never trust him.
He can't be trusted, that he is cruel.
But we never learn that he is so cruel, so bad, absolutely not, because Russia wanted to live in peace with Germany.
They asked him, and they signed it.
So that was the end.
Unless it started in '41.
And then when they took Ukraine, when people start to run from there to our places, where we were, further up, they were telling what's going on.
That's when we learn who is Hitler, what he does to people.
Oh that, first of all, he's a-- he's against Jews.
All Jews were killed in Ukraine as soon as he came, yes?
And that we have to be alert, that he send them-- a lot of people sent from Ukraine to Germany for work.
That's all what they said.
And they run.
My husband was the first to run from Poland to Russia.
That's how he stayed alive.
Yes.
Change?
Look, I was-- I-- I was a patriot, and I wanted to go to army.
But because I was the eldest-- I have a call to army.
But because I was the eldest, my mother went there and said, no, she is the eldest.
My husband now is fighting.
I can't lose my husband and my eldest daughter.
And that's how I stayed with the family.
But when-- we are supposed to be evacuated as soon the army-- Hitler-- come closer to the place we lived.
I mean Krasnodar.
We were-- I was calling on them and ringing them to do something about it, that we are Jewish.
We live in army blocks.
Everybody knows us.
You should evacuate.
We would hear that they're already in [NON-ENGLISH].
They're going very close.
But they said, wait.
When the time come, we will evacuated.
So we wait.
And of course, when [INAUDIBLE] evacuate, it was in 1942.
So we didn't went too far.
About 200 kilometers or 250 kilometers from Krasnodar, we were surrounded.
And not only we, but hundreds of people, thousands of people, hundreds of wagons or by train was stopped.
And [INAUDIBLE]-- they bombed them all.
And we were surrounded by German army.
They took-- they come and said--
--now, you-- you have-- that's army officials come and said, now, you have to get ready tomorrow or day after tomorrow.
I don't remember how they said, but it was very short notice that you have to pack and be ready to evacuate.
So we were ready.
We didn't pack much.
We just left everything that we had.
My father-- we had a grand piano, which my father bought us that we can learn piano.
We left that.
He sold his suit to get us a piano.
And we all played piano.
We left everything behind.
We took a few cases-- suitcases-- and--
--and they come-- they come-- no, no, they come by trucks.
They put us on the trucks, and we went to the tram-- trains.
They put us in wagons.
Of course, normal wagons--
--were sitting-- sitting.
It's not like cattle wagons.
It was normal trains.
And we-- we had our [INAUDIBLE] that was four people.
And they took us.
We went.
We didn't know where we're going [LAUGHS] until they start bomb.
The fire in the beginning is right there.
They stopped all the trains.
And the people ran.
That was approximately in [INAUDIBLE].
Russia.
It's not far-- as I said, it was about 250 kilometers, 300 at the most kilometers, from Krasnodar.
[INAUDIBLE], they start bombed us.
They surrendered the whole thing.
And there we are in the middle of nowhere with nothing.
And it's not only we-- thousands, thousand people run from this.
Some of them died because they-- the bomb went on their wagons.
And if they were not quick enough to get out of wagons, particularly the front one, they all died.
Nobody left.
But we were lucky, because you were in-- in sort of not maybe in the middle, further of the middle of this whole thing.
So we survived.
And with us a lot of people survived.
I remember like this.
A lot of people start runnin'.
And most of them went right.
And I said, Mom, we're going left, because the bombs were still there.
And we went left.
And we walk and walk and walk--
--till we come-- huh?
My sister, my mother-- my two sisters, my mother, and me.
We walk.
And we come [DRINKS] to-- what was that place called?
[RUSSIAN] or something.
Maybe we should stop.
You want [INAUDIBLE].
Yes, [INAUDIBLE].
Somewhere about-- we walked about 50, 60 kilometers-- we come to a club.
I remember club was written.
We went to this club-- not only we, a few families.
It was a big room and we stayed there.
And I remember we still didn't see Germans.
We saw the bombs and things like this.
But we didn't see Germans.
We were staying there about two days or three.
On the third day, somebody said, Germans are here.
And yes, they come.
And they come to the club.
And I don't want even to talk about this, [SOBBING] because from then it started.
Germans come to my sister.
They want to take her.
Then they come to me.
My mother start to scream.
We're all screaming and crying.
The whole club-- [INAUDIBLE] club because they start shooting.
And we thought, oh, that's it.
That's finished.
I didn't know what happened.
I can't remember, but I walked out after the screaming and things like this.
My-- my mother-- like, I-- I-- I-- was like mad.
Mad-- I was running like idiot-- my mother decide-- and screaming.
So-- so-- they run out.
But we didn't-- we stayed overnight.
In the morning, we start run.
We went further and further.
And we come-- OK.
I can [INAUDIBLE] speak.
I can speak, OK.
And then we went further and further.
And we come to a one that was Stanitsa.
In Russia, because it's still where we-- w not that far from the place we live-- we left-- Krasnodar.
No.
It's already goes to next month-- September.
I'm going to talk about August, September, October.
That's-- you see, that's the whole period that we already were in German occupation.
We were already saw them.
We saw the-- the German in August.
But since-- from there on, there were-- we didn't see them often.
But we knew they're there.
And we try to avoid them.
So we walked.
